name: GPU-Accelerated Computing
tagline: Massively parallel computation for graphics, simulation, and ML training
problem:
Some workloads are dominated by linear algebra and parallel operations. CPUs excel at control flow, but they are inefficient for massive parallel numeric throughput.

GPUs solve this by providing many simple cores and high memory bandwidth, enabling feasible training of large models and fast simulation.

The remaining problem for GPU-Accelerated Computing is operational reliability. A reader needs more than a name and a historical note: they need to know what failure this capability prevents, what resources it consumes, what signs show that it is failing, and when it is safer to stop than to improvise. This entry therefore treats gpu-accelerated computing as a teachable system with inputs, inspection points, records, and limits rather than as a single trick.
overview:
A GPU executes many threads in parallel, typically in lockstep groups. Performance depends on memory access patterns, batching, and avoiding divergence. Modern ML relies on GPU kernels for matrix multiplies and attention.

For The Codex, GPUs are an infrastructure node: they require power, cooling, and software stacks, and they shift 'AI from scratch' from theory to practice when combined with datasets and training pipelines.

In collapse recovery, gpu-accelerated computing must be repairable with local materials, teachable to more than one person, and documented well enough that the method survives injury, migration, or leadership change. In a space-settlement context, the same capability must account for closed-loop resources, contamination control, spare-part scarcity, power budgets, and the cost of mistakes in a sealed habitat. The practical minimum is a controlled trial, written checks, safe storage, and review before scale-up.

principles.name: Parallel throughput
principles.explanation: Many simple cores trade latency for throughput on data-parallel tasks.

principles.name: Memory bandwidth
principles.explanation: Bandwidth and locality often dominate performance; compute alone is not the bottleneck.

principles.name: Kernel fusion and batching
principles.explanation: Combine operations to reduce memory traffic and improve utilization.

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what GPU-Accelerated Computing is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

rawMaterials.name: Electric power
rawMaterials.purpose: Energy for computation, storage, and networking
earthLocations: Electrical grids globally; generators in remote sites
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Solar + batteries is the simplest early-space pathway; plan for tight energy budgets and thermal rejection.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Stable voltage regulation, grounding, and basic power-quality control.

rawMaterials.name: Semiconductor devices
rawMaterials.purpose: Transistors and ICs that perform switching, storage, and signal conditioning
earthLocations: Global semiconductor supply chains; packaged IC distributors
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Early colonies import packaged devices; long-term local manufacture requires clean chemistry and crystal growth capabilities.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: ESD-safe handling; correct packaging, heat sinking, and derating for temperature and radiation.

rawMaterials.name: Documentation and standards
rawMaterials.purpose: Make designs reproducible and debuggable across teams and time
earthLocations: Libraries, universities, standards bodies, archived web
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Mirror core references offline; maintain printed and digital copies with checksums and redundancy.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Versioning, change control, and clear measurement units and assumptions.

rawMaterials.name: Cooling capacity
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Radiators and thermal management are first-class constraints; power-limited regimes favor smaller models and efficient inference.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Thermal design, monitoring, and maintenance routines.

buildSteps.title: Provision power and cooling first
buildSteps.description: Ensure stable power delivery, airflow/liquid loops, and monitoring before running sustained workloads.

buildSteps.title: Install runtime stack
buildSteps.description: Use stable drivers and compute libraries; lock versions and document environment.

buildSteps.title: Benchmark kernels
buildSteps.description: Test matrix multiply throughput and memory bandwidth; detect bottlenecks.

buildSteps.title: Train with checkpointing and monitoring
buildSteps.description: Track utilization, temperature, errors, and training stability.

buildSteps.title: Define the local purpose and stop conditions
buildSteps.description: Write down why GPU-Accelerated Computing is needed, who is responsible, what success looks like, and which conditions require stopping or escalation.
buildSteps.warningNote: Do not start a life-critical or infrastructure-critical use without clear stop conditions.

history.event: GPUs shift from fixed-function graphics to programmable compute.
GPU computing becomes central to deep learning.
Specialized accelerators and efficient kernels dominate scaling.

impact:
GPU computing makes modern AI training and simulation feasible and drives a new industrial layer: data centers, cooling, and specialized software stacks.

In The Codex, GPUs are a bottleneck node: without them (or equivalents), large-model training becomes slow or impractical.
